content: A long conducting cylinder having a radius b is placed along the z-axis. The current density is $$\overrightarrow J = {J_a}{r^3}\widehat z$$ for the region r < b where r is the distance in the radial direction. The magnetic field intensity ($$\overrightarrow H $$) for the region inside the conductor (i.e., for r < b) is
explanation:
Given : $$\overrightarrow J = {J_a}{r^3}\widehat z$$

$$\therefore$$ $$I = \int\limits_s {\overrightarrow J \,.\,d\overrightarrow s ;\,d\overrightarrow s = rdrd\phi \widehat z} $$

$$ \Rightarrow I = \int {{J_a}{r^3}\widehat z\,.\,rdrd\phi \widehat z} $$

$$ = {J_a}\int\limits_{r = 0}^r {{r^4}dr\int\limits_{\phi = 0}^{2\pi } {d\phi } } $$

$$ = {J_a}\left. {{{{r^5}} \over 5}} \right|_0^r\,.\,\left. \phi \right|_0^{2\pi } = {{{J_a}(2\pi ){r^5}} \over 5}$$

As $$\oint {\overrightarrow H \,.\,d\overrightarrow L = {I_{enc}} = \int\limits_s {\overrightarrow J \,.\,d\overrightarrow s } } $$

$$ \Rightarrow H(2\pi r) = {{{J_a}(2\pi ){r^5}} \over 5}$$

$$ \Rightarrow H = {{{J_a}{r^4}} \over 5}$$

content: Which one of the following vector functions represents a magnetic field $\vec{B}$ ? ( $\hat{x}, \hat{y}$, and $\hat{z}$ are unit vectors along $x$-axis, $y$-axis and $z$-axis respectively)
explanation:
The vector functions represents a magnetic field $\vec{B}$ if $\mathop{{\int\!\!\!\!\!\int}\mkern-21mu igcirc} {}$ \vec{B} \cdot d s=0$

Where, $\vec{B}=$ Magnetic flux density

By applying Divergence theorem,

$$ egin{aligned} & \iiint_{\vec{ abla}}( abla \cdot \vec{B}) d s=0 \ & \vec{B} \cdot \vec{B}=0 \end{aligned} $$

Checking from options, Option (A) : $\quad \vec{ abla} \cdot \vec{B}=10+0+20=30$ Option (B) : $\quad \vec{ abla} \cdot \vec{B}=10+20-30=0$ Option (C): $\quad \vec{ abla} \cdot \vec{B}=0+0-10=-10$ Option (D): $\quad \vec{ abla} \cdot \vec{B}=0+20+0=20$ $\vec{B}=10 x \hat{x}+20 y \hat{y}-30 z \hat{z}$ represents the magnetic field

Hence, the correct option is (A).

content: The figures show diagrammatic representations of vector fields $$\overrightarrow X,\;\overrightarrow Y,\;and\;\overrightarrow Z$$ respectively. Which one of the following choices is true?

content: A steady current I is flowing in the −x direction through each of two infinitely long wires at $$y=\pm\frac L2$$ as shown in the figure. The permeability of the medium is $$\mu_0$$. The $$\overrightarrow B$$ field at (0,L,0) is

$$\overrightarrow B=\;4x{\widehat a}_x\;+\;2ky{\widehat a}_y\;+\;8{\widehat a}_z\;\;Wb/m^2$$.
The value of constant k must be equal to
Which of the following statements holds for the divergence of electric and magnetic flux densities?

options.content: These are zero for static densities but non zero for time varying densities
It is zero for the electric flux density
It is zero for the magnetic flux density

The magnetic field intensity $$\overrightarrow H $$ at point $$P$$ is

Static magnetic fields induce currents in closed conducting loops.
The line integral of the vector potential A around the boundary of a surface S represents

options.content: flux density in the surface S

content:
The given equation represents a magnetic field strength $\bar{H}(r, \theta, \phi)$ in the spherical coordinate system, in free space. Here, $\hat{r}$ and $\hat{\theta}$ represent the unit vectors along $r$ and $\theta$, respectively. The value of $P$ in the equation should be _________________ (rounded off to the nearest integer).

$$\bar{H}(r, \theta, \phi) = \frac{1}{r^3} ( \hat{r} P \cos \theta + \hat{\theta} P \sin \theta)$$

In the figure, the electric field E and the magnetic field B point to x and z directions, respectively, and have constant magnitudes. A positive charge 'q' is released from rest at the origin. Which of the following statement(s) is/are true.

options.content: The charge will move in the direction of z with constant velocity.
The charge will always move on the y-z plane only.
The trajectory of the charge will be a circle.
The charge will progress in the direction of y.

explanation:
Net force applied on charge, $\vec{F}_{\text {total }}=\vec{F}_e+\vec{F}_m=q \cdot \vec{E}+q \cdot(\vec{V} \times \vec{B})$

Initially charge is at rest. No magnetic force is experienced, due to electric field charge moves in the z direction with increasing velocity. Now because of increasing $v \vec{F}_m$ increases in perpendicular of velocity vector and creates a cycloid trajectory.

content: An infinite surface of linear current density $$K = 5{\widehat a_x}$$ A/m exists on the x-y plane, as shown in the figure. The magnitude of the magnetic field intensity (H) at a point (1, 1, 1) due to the surface current in Ampere/meter is ____________ (Round off to 2 decimal places).
explanation:
$\overrightarrow{H_p}=\frac{1}{2}\left(\vec{K} \times \hat{a}_n\right)$

$\begin{aligned} & \overrightarrow{H_p}=\frac{1}{2}\left(5 \hat{a}_x \times \hat{a}_z\right)=-2.5 \hat{a}_y \\\\ & \left|\overrightarrow{H_p}\right|=2.5 \mathrm{~A} / \mathrm{m}\end{aligned}$

content: If the magnetic field intensity ($$\overrightarrow H $$) in a conducting region is given by the expression, $$\overrightarrow H = {x^2}\widehat i + {x^2}{y^2}\widehat j + {x^2}{y^2}{z^2}\widehat k$$ A/m. The magnitude of the current density, in A/m2, at x = 1 m, y = 2 m and z = 1 m is
explanation:
Given : $$\overrightarrow H = {x^2}\widehat i + {x^2}{y^2}\widehat j + {x^2}{y^2}{z^2}\widehat k$$ A/m

As $$\overrightarrow J = \nabla \times \overrightarrow H $$

$$ \Rightarrow \overrightarrow J = \left| {\matrix{ {{{\widehat a}_x}} & {{{\widehat a}_y}} & {{{\widehat a}_z}} \cr {{\partial \over {\partial x}}} & {{\partial \over {\partial y}}} & {{\partial \over {\partial z}}} \cr {{x^2}} & {{x^2}{y^2}} & {{x^2}{y^2}{z^2}} \cr } } \right| = 2{x^2}y{z^2}{\widehat a_x} - 2x{y^2}{z^2}{\widehat a_y} + 2x{y^2}{\widehat a_z}$$

$$\therefore$$ $${\left. {\overrightarrow J } \right|_{1,2,1}} = 2(1)(2)(1){\widehat a_x} - 2(1){(2)^2}{(1)^2}{\widehat a_y} + 2(1){(2)^2}{\widehat a_z}$$

$$ \Rightarrow \overrightarrow J = 4{\widehat a_x} - 8{\widehat a_y} + 8{\widehat a_z}$$

$$\therefore$$ $$\left| {\overrightarrow J } \right| = \sqrt {{{(4)}^2} + {{(8)}^2} + {{(8)}^2}} = 12$$

content:
One Coulomb of point charge moving with a uniform velocity $10 \hat{x} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ enters the region $x \geq 0$ having a magnetic flux density $\vec{B}=(10 y \hat{x}+10 x \hat{y}+10 \hat{z}) \mathrm{T}$. The magnitude of force on the charge at $x=0^{+}$is

$\_\_\_\_$ N. ( $\hat{x}, \hat{y}$ and $\hat{z}$ are unit vectors along x -axis, y -axis and z -axis respectively)
explanation:
Given : Magnetic flux density, $\vec{B}=(10 y \hat{x}+10 x \hat{y}+10 \hat{z}) \mathrm{T}$

Velocity, $\vec{V}=10 \hat{x} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$

Force, $\vec{F}=(\vec{V} \times \vec{B})$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \vec{F}=(10 \hat{x} \times(10 y \hat{x}+10 x \hat{y}+10 \hat{z})) \\ & |\vec{F}|=|-100|=100 \mathrm{~N} \end{aligned} $$

Hence, the correct answer is 100 .
